Chapter 2
Nutrition
Micah Kohles and Jenna Richardson
To understand the nutrition of pet guinea pigs, it is impor-
tant to consider the diet of their wild counterparts. Guinea 
pigs are herbivorous hystricomorph rodents native to 
South America. Their diet is high in fibre and comprised 
of plant material. In the wild, guinea pigs have access to 
potentially hundreds of species of vegetation, all of which 
differ slightly in nutritional composition and fibre profile 
(Guichón and Cassini, 1998). Guinea pigs select the most 
palatable dietary items. This selection behaviour is an 
effective survival mechanism in the wild but can lead to 
an imbalanced nutritional intake and predisposition to 
disease in captivity. In captivity, guinea pigs are often 
limited to a selection of grains, forages, herbs and vegeta- (a)
bles, which serve as dietary fibre but do not necessarily 
align with what would be available in natural settings.
The recommended diet for a healthy pet guinea pig 
is ad libitum hay (Figure 2.1) and/or grass (guinea pigs 
appear to prefer shorter grass for grazing (Figure 2.2)), 
forage, a large cup of leafy green vegetables once or 
twice daily and a small ration (based on manufacturer 
recommendations and animal specific needs) of a com-
plete uniform pelleted diet that is fortified with stabilized 
vitamin C. Hay and/or grass is the most crucial component 
of the diet. When providing fresh food, a wide selection 
is recommended to avoid the overfeeding of any one 
item. This is particularly important when considering 
(b)
vitamin C and calcium content (see ‘Vitamin C’ and ‘Cal-
cium and vitamin D’, below). Suitable fresh shop-bought  (a, b) Guinea pigs require high-fibre diets and should be 
2.1
and garden greens include: grass, plantain, dandelion,  provided with ad libitum hay. They are well adapted to 
derive energy from fibrous material.
marigold, nasturtium, raspberry leaves, roses (leaves and 
petals), sow thistle, basil, broccoli, cabbage (dark green 
rather than white), carrot tops, celery, chicory, coriander, 
cucumber, dill, fennel, kale, mint, parsley, green and red 
pepper, rocket, spinach, spring greens and watercress. 
Fresh food should always be washed in water before 
feeding to remove any contaminants and to maximize 
water intake.
The average food intake of an adult guinea pig is 60–70 
g/kg per day. However, this can be affected by a multitude 
of physiological factors and increases two- to threefold 
during pregnancy. Overfeeding of rations, especially 
muesli and non-uniform concentrate feeds, with an excess 
of calories can lead to obesity, intestinal bacterial over-
growth or dysbiosis, ileus, and dental disease, as well as 
potentially contributing to behavioural changes (e.g. dis-
interest in eating). Foods high in sugar should also be 
Guinea pigs appear to prefer short rather than long grass 
avoided in order to reduce the risk of dental caries, obesity  2.2 for grazing. This may be due to increased nutritional 
and gastrointestinal bacterial disruption/overgrowth. value and/or palatability.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Muesli-based rations
Research in rabbits has demonstrated that the 
provision of muesli-based concentrate rations leads 
to the development of dental disease. A national 
campaign promoting awareness of this resulted in 
most pet food companies in the UK discontinuing 
muesli-based foods for rabbits and switching to a 
complete uniform pelleted diet. However, despite 
their elodont dentition and similar frequency of 
clinical dental disease, there are still many muesli-
based concentrate diets available for guinea pigs. It is 
widely accepted amongst the veterinary community 
that selective consumption of muesli diets is a major  2.3 Appearance 
of the

It should be noted that while all of these fibre sources 
can be beneficial and important components of a guinea 
pig’s diet they should be incorporated in appropriate 
amounts and formats as part of an overall nutritional profile. 
Digestibility varies with the type and amount of fibre 
(Sakaguchi et al., 1997). It should be noted that fibre is not 
digested by the guinea pig itself but by the microbes 
present in the gastrointestinal tract, predominantly the 
caecum (see also Chapter 15). The caecum is proportion-
ally large (Figure 2.3) and holds approximately 65% of the 
enteric digesta (Barthold et al., 2016). Bacterial fermenta-
tion of plant-based carbohydrates results in short chain 
fatty acids (SCFAs) and volatile fatty acids (acetate, 
butyrate and propionate), estimated to make up 31% of the 
basal metabolic rate (Sakaguchi et al., 1985).
Butyrate has been shown to increase propagating

Guinea pigs absorb calcium through the intestines and

for further information about hypovitaminosis C.
especially if dietary options that are higher in calcium are 
provided. If insufficient water is consumed, more con-
centrated urine is produced with a higher proportion of 
calcium sediment. This can lead to an increased risk of 
urolithiasis (see Chapter 17). This reinforces the impor-
tance of providing numerous different water sources in 
different areas of the enclosure so that guinea pigs can 
select their preferred method and location for drinking 
(see ‘Hydration’, below).
Guinea pigs obtain vitamin D both through their diet 
(plant matter) and via exposure to UVB light (Figure 2.6). 
Commercial guinea pig foods are supplemented with 
vitamin D. Guinea pigs exposed to artificial UVB radiation 
have been shown to have significantly higher serum 
25-hydroxyvitamin D3 concentrations than those housed 
without UVB light provision. The significance of altera-
tions in 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 concentrations has yet to 
be established, largely due to deficient data regarding 
normal values in wild and captive guinea pigs. Further 
Clinical signs of hypovitaminosis can resolve quickly with  research is required to ascertain the health benefits of 
2.5
oral vitamin C supplementation. UVB light supplementation in hystricomorph rodents.

quate opportunity for fermentation.
There are a number of nutritional benefits associated 
with coprophagy. Coprophagy ensures that protein syn-
thesized by bacteria in the caecum and colon is not lost 
via defecation but re-ingested. The protein (nitrogen) 
content of re-ingested pellets is 26–52% higher than that 
of non-ingested pellets and 1.6 times higher than that of 
faecal pellets (Björnhag, 1981). The nitrogen content is 
derived from both the undigested food and the gastroin-
testinal bacteria (Holtenius and Björnhag, 1985). Other 
dietary components, such as vitamins, amino acids and 
undigested remains of essential nutrients (e.g. fatty acids), 
can also be consumed in this way (Franz et al., 2011).
It should be noted that guinea pigs cannot compensate  Some guinea pigs may demonstrate a preference for

via coprophagy (Meyer et al., 2010). Coprophagy benefits

energy intake restrictions (Van Soest, 1996). Prevention of  enrichment

Chapter 2 • Nutrition
Acknowledgement

Iske for her contributions to this chapter.
References and further reading

Environmental enrichment is key to promoting good 
2.9
welfare for pet guinea pigs. (a–d) This enclosure for five 
guinea pigs includes good nutritional provisions, numerous 
hay-filled hides, multiple water sources, absorptive bedding and 
space to exercise.
